comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of the other contender for the title of Japan's Merlin, Kobo Daishi, the founder of the Shingon denomination of esoteric Buddhism. As a young man he embraced Buddhism and engaged in ascetic practices in the mountains, then had a vision in which a spirit told him to seek the Mahavairocana Sutra. When he got it he found that much of it was written in cryptic language and symbolism, and so he traveled to China to find a master to interpret it for him. After many adventures he was initiated by Huiguo, an esoteric Buddhist master, and returned to Japan brimfull of secret teachings. Miracles, magic, and politics duly followed -- yeah, it would make a great fantasy novel. According to Shingon belief he is still alive, having withdrawn into a state of suspended animation to await the coming of Miroku, the Buddha of the Future. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though I'm going to have somewhat limited email access tomorrow and it may be Tuesday or even Wednesday before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.  

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, that means you. See you next week!***

***Ahem.  If you posted a new question after midnight Eastern time, it's been deleted. Please post again next week between midnight Sunday and midnight Monday, Eastern time.***

comments.content:
1) I live a horribly empty, yet abundant in material terms, middle class lifestyle, and every attempt I've made to try to change it has flopped. I've realized part of it is that whenever I do it, I try to change too much at once, and so I want to start make smaller shifts. I'm drawn to Druidry, and so plan to use the holy days as the basis for this. Would starting with spirituality (i.e. SOP and meditation for now) be a good start, or are there things I might need to take care of first?

2) I've been mulling over the recent Trudeau in blackface media explosion, and one of the things which strikes me is that the narrative, of this incident 18 years ago showing us his true nature, only makes sense if people assume he is incapable of change. This assumption that nothing can change seems to show up an awful lot in modern thinking, and when I attempt to press it, I hit an off switch in people's minds, which suggests to me there's something here. I'd never noticed it applied to people before, but it makes sense it would be: it's applied to nearly everything else.

What strikes me is that this explains an otherwise odd phenomenon: the the baffling way so many people, even those who see the problems we face, refuse to do anything about it. It's not a matter of not wanting to, or even of weighing the pros and cons and deciding that they like living this way: it's people literally thinking they're incapable of change.

Does this line of thinking make sense?

3) I've seen the discussion of cacomagic flooding the US here, and realized this might explain something: if the country is flooded with magic aimed to keep people locked in self-defeating behavioural patterns, it follows lots of people should fall into such patterns. We have a word for people locked in such patterns: crazy. Thus, it may be the case that the rest of the world views Americans as somewhat nuts because it's actually true: maybe not always at the individual level, but collectively.

Does this line of thinking make sense?

4) I've been looking into atheist demonolatry, and struck by how insane it is: if they're right no spiritual reality exists, all they're doing is alienating potential allies; while if they're wrong they've put themselves in horrid danger. What seems to make sense of it is if they genuinely hate the Christian god so much that it blinds them. Do you know where such powerful hatred might be coming from?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Choose one thing to do, and set aside everything else for now. I'd recommend the Sphere of Protection. Start work on it, and keep going. Once you've established that as a daily practice, you can go on, but until you pass through the gate of Doing One Thing you won't be able to get anywhere. Give it a try!

2) Yes, that makes sense. The insistence that people can't change is rooted in exactly the experience you mentioned in your first question: you hate your life and yet your attempts to do something else have gone nowhere -- at least so far.

3) I see no lies there. ;-)

4) If the Christian god exists, then the atheists have a great deal of egg on their faces. That can motivate a lot of hatred.

Not the original poster, and not an American, but when I was younger I suffered from having several obsessions about certain members of the opposite sex.

I did some journaling on this recently to try to understand what the source of this was, and it occurred to me that at the time I had no control over my life - no real goals, no ambitions or career direction; a tendency towards binge drinking etc. So really, the obsessions fitted into the general pattern of my life. Therefore the realisation was that if you don't have any control over your life, someone or something else will.

I don't necessarily think (much) cacomagic was involved in my case, because I felt somewhat "lost" from a very early age - I suspect I'm in a very early incarnation with no pre-laid "tracks in space" to follow, and so I'm using the rest of this incarnation to make sure those are in place. replies.content:
On the other hand, I've seen way too many people fall into the opposite trap: believing that everyone deserves enough second chances to fill a wheelbarrow, and you must work toward everyone's redemption even when they show no sign of changing beyond promising they will. (Words are easy: any five-year-old or domestic abuser can cry and say they're sorry, and then do the exact same thing the next day.) Opposite of a bad thing is another bad thing, and all that.

People *can* change. But most don't, because most don't want to--they want credit for trying and an open field full of rubes who give them that credit.

Now, as in Trudeau's case, I think if people *have already* demonstrably changed--especially if the times have changed and they've needed to adjust--and/or they can look back, explain what they did and why they understand it was wrong and won't do it any more, that's a different story. But I'm also as white as they get, so I'm not going to tell a person of color that they shouldn't feel weird about the guy.

tl;dr: Observing demonstrated change is accepting reality, but hoping for change based only on the possibility and/or the claim of the person in question and/or the belief that There's Good In Everyone, Aunt Polly, is a sucker's bet.

My read is that the atheists who exult the devil are trying to do something similar to what JMG has done with the Cthulhu mythos in the Weird of Hali series. Just as Lovecraft's eldritch beasties are an excellent distillation of modern scientistic culture's horror of organic life, these folks view the devil as an amalgamation of Christian orthodoxy's fears of disobedience, free thought, sexuality, nature, the material world, etc. etc. So they try to flip the script, casting the Devil in the role of the benevolent Old Ones and the Church as the dogmatic, authoritarian Radiance. They see Satan/Lucifer as a Promethean archetype, defying religious authority to bring forbidden knowledge to mankind. Those who actually go so far as to do diabolic rituals and prayers may not believe in actual spirits, magic, or psi, but they do believe in the psychological and cultural power of mythic archetypes, and they regard Satan as a fitting symbol of defiance, liberation, and curiosity.

Plus they think it's funny to freak out Christians by displaying pentagrams and Baphomet statues.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Lucifer / Satan as a Promethean figure, and as a hero of free thinkers everywhere, goes very far back in Western culture.  In the later 19th century, one of the most popular free-thought periodicals in the USA had the title "Lucifer, the Light Bearer" (edited by Moses Harman), and Madame Blavatsky's last theosophical periodical was also titled "Lucifer."  LaVey's Church of Satan simply picked up this old idea and ran with it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's unpleasantly likely. Unfortunately, feminism transformed itself over the last half century from a wholly praiseworthy movement for equal legal rights, as it once was, into a cult of entitlement in which women of the middle and upper middle classes were taught to believe that they ought to get everything they want just because they want it.

Whole genres of literature from positive-thinking pop psychology to the bulk of the romance-novel market fixated on the notion that women of those classes deserve whatever they desire, no matter how selfish the desire happens to be. That basically made them easy marks for a whole series of corrupt belief systems, and demonolatry is in many ways the logical endpoint of the progression, since flattering the ego and promising to pander to unfulfilled desires are the basic stock in trade of your common or garden variety demon.

Of course feminism is far from the only belief system that's gone down that road. Atheism, for one, has done much the same thing, and there are others; thus it's by no means exclusive to women of the classes just mentioned, or to feminism. One way or another, I suspect things will get very ugly before that whole trajectory finishes up.

I need some help with geomancy on a very delicate subject.

Before we got together, my fiance had a long, secret relationship with a married man.

Now, this kind of thing ordinarily doesn't concern me. Obviously I don't think it was right-- and neither does she. But the details of my partners' previous relationships has never bothered me very much. I've been with a lot of women, and in my younger days I was not very faithful myself. It's not just that it would be hypocritical for past relationships or sexual experiences to bother me, which it would, it's that I personally, genuinely don't care.

For some reason though, this one does bother me. Moreover, the bother comes in waves-- and during these waves it's very intense, and it feels like a strong pressure near my solar plexus. I sometimes seem to see a dark or reddish energy pushing its way in. I feel intensely jealous, and intensely insecure. I find myself chain smoking cigarettes to calm myself-- when before this, I hadn't smoked in many years and had no intention of doing so again. And it's gone on like this for quite a long time-- in the past even when I have felt jealous, I've gotten over it pretty quickly.

So I started to wonder if something more than just my own insecurity might be involved. Actually she suggested it-- She said she was worried that negative energy was coming at us from outside the relationship. 

So I did some geomancy on the subject, trying to figure out if he is, either intentionally or unintentionally, working hostile magic. She has described him as both very sensitive and very manipulative, and his mother is an energetic healer from a traditional culture; this suggests a natural psychism, possibly not wholly conscious.

I first asked, "Is at least some of the emotional pain I feel around this issue due to [Her Ex]'s influence?" I assumed that this is a 12th house question.

The resulting chart is quite complicated.

In the first house we have Populus, which springs to the 2nd house, the 8th house, and the 9th house. In the 12th house we have Albus, which springs, rather alarmingly, to both the 5th house and the 7th house. 

So for modes of perfection we have a mutation between the 7th house and the 8th house, and also sextiles between the 12th and 2nd and between the 7th and 9th, and a trine between the 5th and the 9th. 

I'm taking this as a "Yes," with the following commentaries:

1. I am described as extremely sensitive and receptive (Populus= Moon in Cancer. Cancer is my rising sign and I am a natural empath.)
2. Something about my magical and religious work (8th and 9th) houses open me to outside influences and relate to this question.
3. He is probably still obsessed with her (Albus in 12 and 7), views me as a rival or enemy (Albus in 7). Albus in 5 also suggests that he spends time staring at her or me on social media. 
4. Albus in 5 is also deeply concerning, as she is currently pregnant with my child.
5. I am certain that she has had no contact with him over any kind in a year. 

So, my questions...

1. Do you agree with my interpretation of the main chart?
2. Do you see anything else I'm missing?
3. What can I do about it?

As an addendum, the other day I was reading the first of your Weird of Hali books (and greatly enjoying it, btw) and I came across the part where Owen discovers that he is under psychic attack. He goes to Laura, who tells him not to fight it, but to hold it as an object of awareness, so that he does not confuse the feelings with himself as the subject of his awareness. Weirdly enough, when I read this, I was experiencing one of these waves of insecurity, and I picked up the book to distract myself. Is this useful advice for a situation like this? I also noticed that she told him to eat to keep his strength up.

Thanks in advance.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This sounds like a pretty classic example of magical attack, and your interpretation seems accurate to me. Fortunately, yes, there's a lot you can do about it. Yes, the material in The Weird of Hali is relevant -- a lot of things are in those books for good reason! An amulet is also very useful, and fortunately you don't need to encounter a Great Old One to make that happen; visit the Magic Monday FAQ and you'll find instructions for making a good protective amulet that will have similar effects to those Owen described. The hoodoo protective bath described in the FAQ is another good resource, and if you're willing to engage in regular ritual practice, the Sphere of Protection ritual covered in previous posts on this journal will protect you if done daily.

There are other things you can do. If your fiance has a photo of her former lover, or anything with his signature on it, that can be put into a bottle with the hottest red pepper you can find and thrown into a river that flows away from the place where the two of you live; go back home by a different route than the one you took to the river. That's a classic way to break a connection with someone you no longer want to interact with.

Any thoughts as to why it lands on me rather than her? (I originally typed this thinking she was the target. Maybe the answer is just "Because I'm the target.")

I'm going to make a new red bag amulet starting as soon as I hit "Post Comment." As for the bottle, if we don't have a river with actual water in it available, does the ocean work?

Finally, regarding my significator in the 8th and 9th houses-- Does that suggest that my meditative work leads to sensitivities that might open me to attack? And is the 8th house as worrisome as I think?

2) Yes, the ocean is also an option. Do it when the tide is going out.

3) Possibly, but more likely the mutation to the 8th simply reflects that there's hostile magic aimed at you.

I had romantic feelings for someone who was a close friend appear out of nowhere a few years ago. We were both in relationships at the time, and so I spent a while wrestling with it, before deciding to end my relationship (it wasn't fair to her that I go through the motions when I had these feelings for someone else). When said close friend asked why I broke up, I explained it to her.

Her response was baffling: she lost it. She bolted out of there, and spent close to a year ignoring me, and telling everyone how horrible I was. She then came back and wanted to talk and try to salvage things, but I decided I didn't want anything to do with her. She recently got in touch again, demanding to know what I'd done, since she knew I was into the occult, and assumed the fact that every single person she's been able to date since then has been similar to me was some form of curse.

What I found interesting here is I realized when she confronted me she's the one person I genuinely hate, and put it together: she probably cast a love spell on me and is stuck with the blowback.

1) Does this sound like the results of a love-spell gone wrong?

2) I keep getting results indicating I can't use a cut and clear spell on her. This is annoying, but I suspect another option to get her out of my life is to help her move on. Do you know ways to do that, or are we stuck with this?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Very much so.

2) There may be karma involved -- that can interfere with cut-and-clear workings. Have you considered using divination to figure out what other options you might have?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 2) I find I can't read open-ended divinations well: I get good results asking what will happen if I do X, and what will happen if I don't do X, but asking what my options are seems beyond my current abilities.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Non-magical advice: tell her to stay away from you. Block her on social media, block her address on email, block her number on your phone. Her decision to move on or not, and her life, is her problem--the old adage "the common factor in all your dysfunctional relationships is you" applies to this particular fruitcake--unless she's messing with your social circle again (in which case give the whole story to your friends, and the ones who are worth keeping up with will make the right call) or acting dangerous (in which case call the police).

I think these fall under "you have to take the necessary steps on the material plane," and from a seen-too-many-relationships-go-sour material perspective, the best way to get someone out of your life is not to let her back into any part of it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I ran into her in the neighborhood and she made a scene: I've already blocked every way for her to get in touch with me, and pretty well all my friends know she's a little nuts, even if they don't know exactly what's going on (since I don't keep tabs on her); I listened to what she had to say, and then said in no uncertain terms I still want nothing to do with her.

Sometimes, people just won't go away....

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ooof, yeah. My sympathy.

My suggestion: next time, don't even listen. If you see her on the street, employ what Regency society called "the cut direct"--pretend you don't see her, lift your chin, and walk on by. If she makes a scene, keep walking. It'll feel deeply uncomfortable and conspicuous, but any bystanders will pretty clearly see that *she's* the crazy one. (If there are convenient police officers/train conductors/etc, by all means approach them if she follows you.)

Basically, in addition to what JMG might recommend, I advise pretending she doesn't exist as hard and fast as possible, and hoping that she takes the hint. (Or that the universe does and she falls down a well, but I'm mean.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) In the vast majority of cases, what you think about the afterlife has zero effect on what afterlife you get. The universe is under no obligation to cater to our notions! A materialist who dies goes through the same experiences as other people, but without the advantage of help from gods or spirits, and with no way of making sense of what's happening.

They're persons and should be treated as such, but they will certainly bestow virtues on those who pray to them, and some are associated with cosmic principles.

Assuming the planes in Hermetic tradition, where are will and consciousness stored?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"Stored" in what sense? They don't go on shelves, you know. ;-)

Will and consciousness are the two basic qualities of the self, and they exist and function at all levels. As soon as your soul drew itself together out of the raw material of soul-stuff and became able to take on a body, you had a very simple will and a very limited consciousness, which enabled you to exist as a single-celled organism. Life after life, you developed a more complex will and a more enlarged consciousness. At first you were only aware of the material plane; then you became aware of higher planes, a little at a time. Now you're a human being; you have a will and consciousness that have reached the point where you can will to will and be conscious of being conscious; you are aware of the material plane (through your senses), the astral plane (through your imagination), and a little of the mental plane (through your intellect), and you can also act on all of these. As you move beyond the human level your will and consciousness will unfold further, but they're always with you -- in a real sense, they are you.

1) It really depends on your circumstances. Ritual work is almost infinitely compressible -- if you can turn around, for example, you can do a modified banishing ritual or SoP -- and meditation can be done in any position. If it gets interrupted, go back to it later and pick up where you left off. Make whatever modifications the circumstances require, remembering that the intention is the core and the physical motions are peripheral.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

A bunch of connected issues are coming up while working with the SOP to balance qualities within myself.

Last week you suggested dealing with self-centeredness coupled with a desire for approval by invoking Fire for confidence and a clear sense of self-worth. After going through several iterations with other elements in the working, an Ogham reading finally said to proceed, so I've been doing the SOP with this working for the past couple of days. So far, I've been presented with situations in my day to day life where I would normally respond with qualities I am trying to banish, so it looks like the working is working!

One thing that has become clear is that I don't know what a clear sense of self-worth actually looks like, I am not sure if I ever experienced it! Or perhaps I only notice periods in which there is a lack or an abundance of a sense of self-worth. A clear sense implies a realistic sense of self-worth, but I'm struggling to imagine what this must be like.

What got me a little closer was meditating on the difference between the passions and desires (will) and the emotions (between Fire and Water). I rather surprisingly never made the basic distinction that both involve feelings, but desire and passion-based feelings come from internal aspects relating to the self's will, whereas emotions are feelings which come as responses to things and events external to the self.

As you've said before, self-centeredness and a desire for approval from others are two sides of the same coin, and if I'm right with the above, they come from an excess of Fire/will, such that the will is focused too much on the self and the act of willing, and wants for the sake of wanting. I notice this in a practical sense in active daydreaming I sometimes engage in (a habit from childhood) where I imagine myself doing something incredibly well in front of other people, which is perhaps the source of my problems with confidence.

In general the SOP is helping me a great deal, despite the great difficulty in untangling what comes up in meditation, and I see connections in other areas of life: I just finished reading Hermann Hesse's novel Narcissus and Goldmund, and he touches on these topics by personifying combinations of traits (symbolized by the elements in Druidry) through the two central characters. A lot of things clicked by reading that book while practicing the DMH and meditating.

Anyhow, just a report on how things are going.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Excellent! This kind of thing is always slow going, and especially so when you're having to overcome the momentum of our culture, which goes all out to keep people from developing a reasonable sense of self-worth -- after all, people who have that can't be tricked into buying an endless stream of shoddy consumer products in an attempt to shore up an inflated and unstable ego (the usual result of the fixation on "self-esteem" so common these days). I'm delighted, though not surprised, to hear that Hesse was helpful -- his novels are among the most psychologically profound works of fiction I know. (Of course he and Carl Jung were good friends, so that figures...)

comments.title: Natal Astrology
comments.content:
I have several astrology questions for you this week, JMG, following on last week's discussion about planetary affliction and talismans.

1) I took a stroll through my natal dignities and debilities per your outline of essential and accidental affliction, and it looks like I could work with these planets in this order, starting with the most dignified:

The Sun - In Rulership and Triplicity in Leo, in the mid-heaven, trine Neptune, sextile Pluto, and only in opposition to a very weak Jupiter.

Mars - Physically in Aries, also ruling the Ascendant in Scorpio (or is that Pluto?), trine Moon, (also trine Lilith, but I'm glossing over the minor players for now), sextile Saturn, but square Mercury, and opposite Uranus, a "T-square" I think you called that.

Saturn - in his triplicity in Gemini, sextile Mars, but square Pluto.

Those are my strongest three. None of them reside in angular houses. Do I have them in the correct order?

2) I'm not ready to just yet, but if I wanted to make a talisman am I right to lean toward a Sun talisman in Aries to align with his exaltation, in order to bring balance and harmony to all that solarly solarness?

3) I also did a quick run-through on the charts of my other family members. My wife's chart is nearly free of affliction - no fall, no detriment, no retrogrades, with plenty of accidental dignity, and fairly few accidental afflictions.

What does this say, if anything, about a person? I mean, I think she's pretty perfect, but...

4) The one thing that bothers me about my wife's chart only bothers me in combination with the charts of my two children. Her Mars is combust (in Virgo, in VII, and square Neptune, her most debilitated planet). This isn't her Sun sign or ascending ruler, but could her Mars relate to me? Especially considering...

5) Both of my children have strong ruling planets - Venus in the case of my daughter, Mercury in the case of my son - both very well-dignified, BUT both combust again.

Because of my over-the-top solary solarness I can't help but think this could point out an issue with me, for all of them. Tell me I'm worrying unnecessarily?

6) For Mercury and Venus only I guess, does it matter which side of the Sun they're on in combust? IOW, is the combustion effect lessened at all if they are physically on our side of the local star?

7) Unlike my wife's chart, my son's chart is highly afflicted. Like, there's a whole square, between the Sun, Saturn, Pluto, and then Jupiter and Uranus in conjunction together on the 4th corner of the square. Which also obviously creates a lot of oppositions to boot.

In fact, only Mercury, in Gemini at mid-heaven, and ruling the ascendant in Virgo, is probably strong enough for him to work with one day. All the other planets are moderately to SEVERELY afflicted, including the Moon (he's a solar Cancer) in her fall in Scorpio and square Venus, a Saturn with 3 squares and 2 oppositions (an arrangement I've dubbed a T-square squared!), the Sun in Cancer with 3 squares and an opposition, Pluto with 3 squares and an opposition, Jupiter and Uranus in tight conjunction in Aries, both with 2 squares and an opposition.

Hoo boy. Is that as bad as it looks? He was overdue and my wife was miserable and induced labor (which I wasn't happy about), and now seeing this, she feels dreadfully guilty about landing him in this position. I tried to reassure her that all that squaring and opposition was with the outer planets, who were unlikely to have moved much in the next week or so. But the Sun might have moved enough to break his half of the square loose, and perhaps that's why he was running late for his due date. Any advice for her? Or for my son, in working with a chart like that?

Thank you so much!
Tripp

replies.title: Re: Natal Astrology
replies.content:
Okay, relax, take a deep breath, and have a beer. Astrology is not about being doomed. Squares are sources of strength as well as of challenges, and having planets in weak placements simply means that your son's going to have to work a little harder than other people. I've got a serious T-square in my chart and a bunch of planets in difficult positions, not to mention a combust Mercury, and I still managed to survive. ;-)

You might tell your wife that every soul seeks an appropriate life when it comes into incarnation, and details like a sudden decision to induce labor can be part of the process whereby the soul gets the conditions it needs. Clearly your son's soul decided to do some heavy lifting in this life, and her decision helped him get the conditions he needs to do that.

Given your chart, a solar talisman would be very effective -- even in opposition, Jupiter's not very troublesome. Don't get it until you're ready to run for public office, though. Sun well dignified conjunct the midheaven is a typical position for elected officials, and if you amplify it you're probably going to be mayor before you know it. Mars and Saturn are also options, but both of those can be challenging to work with, so be sure to do serious divinations before you get or make talismans for them.

comments.title: Local shops verses chains
comments.content:
I have managed a complete avoidance/boycott of a certain multinational burnt coffee chain since June, in an effort to force myself to try out local coffee places.

I stepped inside one of these chains this last week for the first time in four months, and I had the strangest gut instinct feeling. The place was smarmy, dark and negative. My friends had been having fun and joking around at a (local) cafe just before, but once we got inside this chain they started complaining and getting grumpy.

I didn't buy anything because I intend to keep up with my promise, but even just sitting there made me feel uncomfortable. Not only did I feel like a recovering alcoholic sitting in a bar, but the atmosphere weighed on me.

1. What do you think this is? I don't consider myself "sensitive" in the least, and I have only been doing spiritual practice since the start of the year, just the DMH-SOP with Ogham & meditation.

2. Do you think all huge chains are like this, and I just can't tell because I'm still there often enough? Could it be the collective karma of the company, that of gaming the system to run small places out of business, or is this feeling something the customers bring in, that this place attracts grumpy complainers? I know that this shop actually has a manager & franchise owner who's a nice guy and treats his employees well, because a friend used to work there.

3. As an exercise of will, how long do you think I should avoid a place until I feel "safe" that it won't tempt me anymore? I was thinking six months before I add another chain to this list, or do you think I should stick to one place per year?

Thank you for your time, and Happy Autumnal Equinox and Alban Elued.

-Pondering

replies.title: Re: Local shops verses chains
replies.content: That's utterly fascinating. No, I don't think all chains are like that; I think you're perceiving the energetics of that specific chain. I don't drink coffee -- it gives me migraines -- and prefer plain honest green tea to the gussied-up garbage that burnt-coffee vendors sell, so I've been to one of those places once in the last ten years...but you know, it wasn't a pleasant experience, and that may be because of the same thing you sensed. As for how long to stay away, if the place gives you a bad vibe, why not stay away from it permanently?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There's an enormous amount of outright Paganism in Picatrix, enough so that the author gets very nervous about it at one point! The Olympian gods were equated with the planetary spirits long before Christianity showed up -- Walter Burkert talks about this in the last chapter of his fine book Greek Religion -- and the religion of Picatrix, like that of the Renaissance European magi, was the same religion practiced by late classical Pagan Neoplatonists such as Proclus: reverence for a supreme divine principle, the One, combined with traditional worship of a busy pantheon of gods that were seen as subordinate to the One, and which extended all the way from the highest gods to minor local spirits and heroes, including angels and much more on the way.

replies.title: Re: two questions
replies.content:
1) There's a whole slew of exercises that will teach you to sense etheric energy. One of the simplest is to shake your hands out thoroughly, and then hold them out to your sides, the arms not quite straight, the fingers spread but not stiff. Breathe in and out slowly seven times, imagining that you're breathing through the skin of your hands. Then bring your hands together in front of you, maybe a foot apart, cupping them slightly as though holding a ball. Imagine the ball as vividly as possible while you take seven more slow deep breaths. Then move your hands very slightly toward and then away from each other, paying close attention to what you feel. Do this once a day; most people find that within a couple of days at most, they feel something like a pressure against their hands. That's etheric energy.

2) You can always phrase things in terms of psychology, which after all is much of what's going on in affirmations. "Dear, I'd like to do something to help us get along better" (or whatever it happens to be). "You know those sentences you repeat to get your mind in a better state? That sort of thing. Do you mind?" And go from there. Yes, I think it's basic courtesy to ask before praying for someone, unless you know that they'll welcome it -- for example, if they belong to the same religion you do.

comments.title: Demons and Self Annihilation
comments.content:
Last week in a conversation on the Qlippoth, you said “The survival of the Qlippoth is in its own way an act of mercy: those beings who are unable to reintegrate themselves with the whole during the lifespan of a universe can be held over to the next great cycle and try again.”

Two questions on this:

1: How can this conception of the Qlippoth and the concept of the self-annihilation of the black mage be reconciled against each other? It seems that in the first case, any being that set out on the sort of magical path that should lead to self-annihilation should instead get more and more deeply mired in the demonic realms until the cosmic cycle ends and then become a Qlippoth in the next one.

Meanwhile, if self-annihilation were possible, it seems that the beings that would be on the road to becoming Qlippoth would nix themselves long before they ever had a chance to get to that state.

To resolve the binary I could see the Qlippoth themselves as being on a course of slow decay that if not altered ends in annihilation, but it seems that the being that reached that point would be as far removed from the equivalent of a human state of existence as the spirits of planets and stars are, just in the opposite direction. The way you’ve described the concept of self annihilation though makes it sound like something that can be done from the human (or equivalent) phase of development, (but that sucks things back into the loop of contradictions above).

2: The concept of the Qlippoth seems to get used in two very different ways, on one hand as a state of initial imbalance that precedes every act of creation which is brought into order with the initial descent of creative force symbolized by the lightning flash. The other is in reference to actual demonic entities that predate this cycle of creation. One seems inevitable and impersonal, just the particular flavor that imbalance takes with every emanation which can potentially unfold in the actions of individual beings, while the other describes actual beings capable of willed individual action.

What is the relationship between these two concepts? By using the same name for the beings and the states of being, is the implication that the states of being are not in fact, impersonal cosmic forces but the intentional creation of individual beings that pre-date the cosmos? Or is it a matter of alignment, saying that the imbalanced forces are cosmic constants but that the beings that sink to that level are inevitably locked in a state of correspondence so strong it becomes a state of identification? If it’s a matter of correspondence, then why would the same word be used for both things?

replies.title: Re: Demons and Self Annihilation
replies.content: These are teachings from two different traditions within occult philosophy, and of course there are going to be difficulties fitting them all together. You might consider approaching these matters in the same spirit that Dion Fortune asks readers to approach The Cosmic Doctrine, as things meant to train the mind rather than inform it. Approach the universe of your experience in both these ways. What do they teach you?

replies.title: Re: Solar Plexus
replies.content:
1) Sitting in an easy chair or lying on a bed, draw a deep breath, filling your chest. Now, while holding the breath, push it down so that your belly bulges out and your chest flattens. Push it back up to your chest. Repeat this three times and then let the breath out completely, and go limp, relaxing your body as completely as you can. Breathe normally for a couple of minutes, then repeat the exercise. Do it a total of three times, and do this once a day.

2) I don't know of a way to do so -- but then computer gimmickry is not my strong suit.

replies.title: Re: Masons

Freemasonry in Europe is a mixed bag. Most European Masons are like American Masons -- guys who like to devote time to charity, who enjoy ornate rituals, and who try to live up to a fairly demanding ethical code. Europe lacks the sort of strict Grand Lodge system found all over the English-speaking world, though, and so from time to time you get renegade lodges that get into various kinds of unpleasant conduct -- the sort of thing that in the US will get you flung out of Masonry so hard your rump will leave a dent in the sidewalk.

replies.content:
Karma normally expresses itself through character. If you're a jerk, you will behave in ways that reinforce being a jerk, and people will treat you like a jerk, until you get tired of slamming your face into that brick wall and start to change how you treat other people.

Discursive meditation is a good way to stop being a jerk because it teaches you to reflect on things, and once you pick up the habit of reflective thinking, you're much more likely to reflect on the fact that acting like a jerk will make other people treat you as such. Divination is helpful because your daily reading will likely tell you "don't be such a jerk" over and over again, in various colorful ways, until it sinks in.

comments.title: Satuette

The statuette is a thanks gift to Fortuna because I had a terrible accident and came within a whisker of gouging out my right eye. Besides a cut on both lids, I had no permanent damage.

replies.title: Re: Satuette
replies.content:
You don't need to consecrate the statue, but it's a good idea to welcome her by burning incense before the statue -- that's the classic Roman act of reverence. Frankincense is the most traditional incense to use. You can also repeat the prayer Ave Fortuna Dea ("Hail to the goddess Fortune"; it's pronounced AH-way for-TOO-na DAY-a) when you do so. As for books on Roman religion, I don't know of any that focus on the worship -- anyone else?

Glad to hear that you're okay, btw.

replies.title: Re: Satuette
replies.content:
I have been looking for some time for a statuette of Tyche in the classical style. From Her attributes, she seems to related to Fortuna--my favorite photo in a book shows her with a mural crown and standing atop a ship. The main distinction between them from the limited reading I have done is that Tyche is particularly in charge of the fortunes of city states (and by extension, nations and their governments).

Also I have the impression that her favors tend to be arbitrary--luck rather than anything earned.

I would like to have Tyche represented on my altar because I want to make offerings to Her on behalf of my country. The Ancient Providence that has preserved the United States through many grave crises where ordinary fallible human beings were falling short may, I think, be getting ready to divorce us on account of bad behavior or irreconcilable differences.

I can't find any dignified classical style Tyche statuettes in the stores or on websites. The popular ones are aimed at gamblers and are sexed up as male fantasies of Lady Luck.

If your very talented niece has any interest is making a cast of the Fortuna she sculpted, and cast it in some affordable material (pottery, pot metal, poured stone), I would be Customer #1. If that isn't on, perhaps a photo or sketch of it if she is willing? Or if she is reluctant to share her personal art with a total stranger, I would be interested in links or references to the images that inspired her, and I'll endeavor to print my own image.

replies.title: Re: Privilege help...
replies.content: The old concept of noblesse oblige -- basically, that being privileged brings with it the obligation to help those less privileged -- is crucial to cultivate if you have privilege. Are there arrangements in the less affluent communities nearby that get food to those who need it? Support them, and not just in token ways. Are there other ways you can be of service? Do them. Remember always that you are no better than the people who are struggling to get by in rundown council housing -- you just happened to be born in a more privileged class, and got advantages they didn't have. That doesn't require you to go around with that insufferable hangdog air that afflicts so many people these days; your privilege is nothing to be ashamed of, just as it's nothing to take pride in -- it's simply the luck of the draw. It's how you use it that, to use an old-fashioned turn of phrase, distinguishes the true gentleman or lady from the scoundrel and the cad.

comments.title: Baptism and Multiple Jesii
comments.content:
If I understand previous posts correctly, you have suggested that those who voluntarily undergo baptism are in some way connected with the Christian God. This connection persists even if the individual follows other gods and goddesses or even none at all.

However, in other posts you have shared the theory that there are multiple beings going by the name Jesus. Some of them are kind and loving, and some not so much.

From a polytheistic perspective, would you see God the Father, God the Holy Spirit, and God the Son (or Sons, in this case) as separate beings? To what being would the baptized be connected?

Thank you.

Christopher Kinyon

replies.title: Re: Baptism and Multiple Jesii
replies.content: That's a really interesting question to which I'm far from sure I have the answer. I tend to take Christian theology at face value when they say their god has three personae -- the word literally means "masks," and it's entirely reasonable that one god could manifest to human beings in three different ways. A baptism in proper form would create some kind of connection to that threefold being -- but you're right that there's an interesting question raised by the stunning diversity in Jesii, from the god of unconditional love worshiped by some Christians to the spirit of hatred and self-righteousness pretty clearly worshiped by others.

replies.title: Re: Baptism and Multiple Jesii
replies.content:
If I may: for many (most?) Christian denominations, the validity of the sacrament of baptism does not depend on the spiritual state or understanding of the person performing the baptism.

This at least suggests that the Christian Triune God has a claim on the baptized individual, even if the officiant was worshiping one of the "other Jesii".

replies.title: Re: Baptism and Multiple Jesii
replies.content: “Satan often comes disguised as an angel of light.”

replies.title: Re: Baptism and Multiple Jesii
replies.content:
IIRC Christian doctrine is that the Trinity is simultaneously three separate entities and a single entity? Wouldn't surprise me if they're correct about that, with the answer depending on which level you look at. I think there might be polytheist precedent, too: IIRC both the Hindu Brahma-Vishnu-Shiva trinity and the various maiden-mother-crone triune goddesses like the Fates and Norns have similar interpretations (though admittedly that could just be interpretations by people drawing from the Christian Trinity in an inappropriate context). If that's not the case, I'd weakly lean towards the Trinity being separate entities; the Father and Son seem to be drawing off of different archetypes.

At any rate, I'm pretty sure the Father has the same problem as the Son when it comes to different entities answering to the same name, and may actually have it worse: there's at least one archetype expression (Abusive Father - Abused Son) that hits the Father much more heavily than the Son. (I suspect that's one of the issues fueling child abuse in tainted church egregores.)

I'm not sure about the Son, but I'm pretty sure baptism makes a connection to the Father, and that can manifest in rather ugly ways - my standing hypothesis, given one experience a few years back, is that there's at least one entity that would call itself the Father that sees baptism as *drowning*. (The Eucharist is the other way around; I'm not sure about the Father, but I'm pretty sure it's connected to the Son. I accidentally touched that while ~meditating a couple of years back; I was considering a mental image of Jesus that would generally be viewed as quite heretical (a mortal saint in the Buddha mold, who saw the enormity of suffering in the world and tried to do something to point a way out) and felt a *massive* current of force behind it, focused on the Eucharist as the something in question. Not my current - it felt like being suspended in a river that I was not meant to travel down - but quite powerful nonetheless.)

(The Holy Spirit is a considerably more interesting question; It strikes me as being different from the other two members of the Christian Trinity, and I'm not at all sure what to make of that.)

- pretentious_username

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The thing to keep in mind is that taking the road to sustainability we almost took in the 1970s would have required a great deal of hard work without much short term reward. It would have required the same kind of general mobilization that helped win the Second World War, with some items being rationed, others not being available at all, and a lot of wants and cravings put on hold for the time being to free up the resources that would have been needed. When Reagan and his corporate backers came on the scene insisting that we didn't have to do that, that it was "morning in America" and the future didn't matter, people bought into that because it was so much easier and more enjoyable to sell out. Once the choice was made, of course, the psychology I analyzed in an old blog post came into play.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
For me, it was somewhat complicated. When I entered kindergarten (age 5) and first encountered a teacher in the classroom, I realized for a certainty that I had to become a teacher. Eventually I came to the top of the usual educational ladder, with a PhD, and then I did become a teacher/professor at a university. This did not take much effort on my part, and I virtually fell into (or was shoved by unseen forces into) the position that I held throughout my entire academic career at my university. So that much of the answer to your question was fairly straightforward, and much like our host's answer.

However, there was another side to all this as well: what was I meant to teach? I moved through five or six different (though somewhat related) academic fields during my career at that university, which was--in those years--almost unique in allowing a professor to teach courses in departments other than his own, or even outside of any department. These changes in academic field had mostly to do with changing patterns of student enrollment in various departments, and in various academic fields and sub-fields. And there seemed to be neither rhyme nor reason to any of them; but they pushed me here and there apparently at random.

Then, when I was 50, and was one of five professors co-teaching a course that served as an Introduction to Medieval Studies, something changed. I had been doing the introductory framework for the course (defining the Medieval World chronologically and geographically, and outlining its main common feratures); but I had become somewhat bored with doing that. So I and another of the five professors switched, and I found myself with a 2.5-week block of classes that I could devote to some other topic of interest to students. Richard Kieckherfer's superb book, "Magic in the Middle Ages," had just been published, and on a whim I used it as the basis for my block of classes.

Student interest was so strong, and feedback so positive, that I launched a new course on "Magic in the Middle Ages" in the following academic year. As I taught it, it began to feel like I finally knew what I had always been meant to do, and for which I had been preparing myself--all unawares--since my boyhood. And so it was. I taught that, and another course on women-led magical religions in the US from 1770 to 1970, until I retired.

And everything--absolutely everything!--I had ever studied and taught up to that point turned out to be useful for me as I was teaching those two courses.

So the other side to your question is that you may not find out the full answer until much, much later in your life; but you may be led toward that answer by a very circuitous route that will make little sense for decades. If that fits you, then trust in the process even though you can't understand yet where it is headed.

comments.title: Catholic Upbringing
comments.content:
With the recent talk of Baptism and what it does to you spiritually, I have to wonder the same for Confirmation in the Catholic Church. For those unaware, Confirmation is one of the seven sacraments of the Catholic Church. It is also one of the three sacraments of initiation into the Catholic Church, the other two being Baptism and Holy Communion.

Growing up in a rather devout Roman Catholic household, I've participated in all three of the sacraments of initiation. The Church never really felt like home though, and doing Communion and Confirmation were simply things that I was expected and "forced" to do. I saw forced in "" because it wasn't cruel or unusual punishment for me or anything like that, simply that I would be severely disappointing my parents by refusing. And being a quiet and meek kid, it seemed simpler to endure an hour of church than fight with my parents.

I know that I am tied to the Christian God via my baptism, but what further spiritual ties have I made by the other two? I find myself far more drawn now to druid polytheism, and wonder if these have made anything more difficult for myself.

replies.title: Re: Catholic Upbringing
replies.content: None of that has to make things difficult for you! It simply means that there's a connection. If you went through confirmation under duress, and not because you actually wanted to affirm the link formed at baptism (which is what that ceremony is meant to do), it won't have done much; the connection is there, and you can choose to work with it or not.

comments.content:
As a brief, late-in-the-day comment on astral conditions in the United States...

The following occurred to me recently.

There is a certain type of more-than-usually awful person that I've encountered many times and that most of us will encounter, I think, at least once. This sort of person is often charming at first, but is full of stories about how hard their life has been. It seems they've been fired recently, which was just like the last time they got fired; their girlfriend or boyfriend just dumped them, the same way that the one before that did, and the one before that; the landlord is threatening to evict them, through no fault of their own-- they told him the rent would be late this month-- and when they took their car in to get serviced last month the lousy automechanic charged them double. And if you could just lend them five hundred dollars, or a thousand-- or if you could just get them a job-- or if you could just let them stay here tonight, or for the rest of this week-- well, once they're back on their feet everything will be fine again. It seems they've suffered a great deal-- always at someone else's hands. And they always need something from you. Money, a job, a place to crash, a car to borrow. And if you aren't familiar with the type, or if they're more than usually good, you will find yourself sucked in. You'll lend them the money or help them get the job, only to find that next week it's something else, and something else, and something else. Finally, after a week or a month or longer, depending on your patience and level of gullibility, you tell them to get lost-- And you realize, as you regather yourself and take stock of the damage they've caused and try to make sense of things, that they're now going to move on to another victim, and that when they tell the story of you, you're going to be yet another name in the long list of people that have screwed them.

Such people are quite common. They very often have problems with drugs or alcohol, though not always; their primary qualities are their fixation on their own victimhood and their demands that everyone else pay for them. If you make it to adulthood, you usually figure out how to spot people like this a ways off and draw boundaries before they can do any damage. You might wish them well or hope they can get treatment, but you certainly don't believe their victim stories, or believe that they're entitled to your money, your couch, or a job at your company.

Looking at it from a more esoteric perspective, people like this act as psychic vampires. And addiction problems are very often spiritual in nature. (There is a reason that the word "obsession" means both the mindset of an addict and "to be tormented by demons.")

Now it occurs to me that this exact same narrative, of simultaneous victimhood and entitlement, is the same that is pushed 24/7 by both ends of the political spectrum, using means such as television which we've talked about before here as being wholly magical in nature. Might that have something to do with the toxic astral conditions, here?
